title: Chapter 7: The Red Thread
content: The archive breathed cold.

Jane had been in it for three hours, alone with the biodegradable silk collection — forty-seven garments arranged on padded hangers beneath climate-controlled muslin, each one a piece of fashion history. This was the series that had made Thornfield. Vogue had called it "the death of waste." The New York Times had run the label above the fold. Every major museum had requested loans within weeks of the debut, and Edward Rochester had declined them all. The collection stayed here, in the stone-walled belly of his château, like a king hoarding gold.
Jane understood hoarding. She understood the impulse to keep beautiful things close, to touch them when no one was watching. But she wasn't here to touch. She was here to read.

She worked methodically, lifting each garment from its hanger with cotton-gloved hands, laying it flat on the archive's single felt-covered table, and photographing it from every angle — front, back, side, interior. Her phone filled with images she would organize later, cross-referenced against the construction notes she kept in her head. The light was institutional, flat and colorless, the kind that made everything look like evidence. That suited her fine.

The garments were extraordinary. The biodegradable silk had a luminosity that standard silk couldn't achieve — an organic drape that moved like water, like skin, like something still alive. The dyes were plant-derived, subtle gradations of cream and gold and sage that shifted depending on the angle. And the construction: flawless. Every seam invisible, every edge self-finished, every junction resolved with a precision that bordered on obsession.
It was the same hand.

She had known it before she opened the third garment, before she traced the shoulder seam of the fifth, before she held the eleventh up to the light and watched the fabric glow like a lantern. The same hand she had identified days ago — the hand that moved beneath Edward Rochester's name like a river beneath ice. The hand that constructed while he received the credit. She had come to the archive expecting confirmation, and the garments delivered it in spades.
But confirmation wasn't the same as understanding. She could name the hand. She could trace its signature in every seam. She still couldn't explain why it hid.

She reached for garment number twenty-three: a column dress in pale gold, floor-length, with a cowl neckline that fell like a prayer. It was one of the simpler pieces in the collection — deceptively so. Simplicity in couture was the hardest trick of all. There was nowhere to hide a mistake.
Jane turned it inside out.

The interior of a garment was its confession. The outside performed; the inside told the truth. She had learned this at her mother's knee, turning vintage Dior inside out on the cutting table, her mother's nicotine-stained fingers tracing the architecture of boning channels and hand-rolled hems. "Anyone can wear a dress," her mother said. "Reading one takes a lifetime."

Jane's gloved fingers moved across the interior seams. The side seams were clean — French seams, as expected, the raw edges entirely enclosed. The armhole was set with tiny, even prick stitches, each one a perfect anchor. The hem was miraculous: a single line of silk thread so fine it vanished into the fabric, joining two layers without visible evidence that they had ever been separate.
Then her fingers paused.
A seam that shouldn't be there.

It ran along the left side of the bodice, parallel to the princess seam but offset by two centimeters — close enough to look deliberate, far enough to serve no structural purpose. It didn't shape the garment. It didn't close an opening. It wasn't a reinforcement or a repair. It simply existed, a line of stitching in a garment where every line was accounted for and this one had no reason.
Jane reached for her loupe.

She kept it on a cord around her neck — a jeweler's loupe, ten-power magnification, the same one she'd used since her first apprenticeship. She held it to her eye and bent over the seam, and the world collapsed to a circle of cream and gold and—
Red.

A thread. A single strand of red cotton, wound through the seam in a pattern that had nothing to do with construction. Against the cream silk interior it was startling, almost violent — a drop of blood on parchment. Jane's breath stopped. Her fingers continued, tracing the thread through the loupe, following it as it wound in and out of the fabric in a rhythm that was too regular to be accidental.
Dots and dashes.

Her mind raced ahead of her fingers. She had learned Morse code in an oxfam bookshop in Shoreditch, of all places — a dog-eared manual wedged between a sewing guide and a field diary from 1943. She'd thought it was curiosity, the kind of useless knowledge that accumulated in the corners of an obsessive mind.
She read it now: dot-dot-dot-dot. H. Dot. E. Dot-dot-dash-dot. L. Dot-dash-dash-dash. P.
HELP.

Jane set the garment down. The silk whispered against the felt, a sound like a held breath releasing. She stood in the cold archive with the word burning behind her eyes, and her hands did not shake. Her hands never shook. Her mind shook — a seismic event, tectonic plates of understanding grinding against each other — but her hands were steady.
She reached for garment number twenty-four.

A cocoon coat in ivory and cream. She turned it inside out. The seams were perfect, flawless, the same immaculate hand. She examined each one through the loupe, and there, hidden in the fold of the back shoulder seam: red thread. Dots and dashes. HELP.
Garment twenty-five. A slip dress in champagne silk. HELP.
Garment twenty-six. A wrap top in bone white. HELP.

Jane moved through the collection like a woman possessed, pulling each piece from its hanger, inverting it, scanning the seams. The red thread appeared in every single garment — never in the same position, never in the same seam, always hidden in the architecture. A stitch here, a stitch there, always red cotton against cream and gold and ivory, always spelling the same four letters.
HELP. HELP. HELP. HELP.

Dozens of garments. Dozens of messages. Each one stitched by the same hand — the hand that built the collection, the hand that worked in secrecy, the hand that had been crying out from inside the seams of Thornfield's crown jewel for years.
This wasn't whimsy. This wasn't art. This wasn't a designer playing with hidden messages, encrypting her initials or signing her work in secret. This was a prisoner tapping on the pipes.
Jane sat back on her heels.

The stone floor was cold through her trousers. The archive held its temperature like a cave — fourteen degrees, humidity controlled, designed to preserve fabric. It preserved silence just as well. The only sound was the faint hum of the climate unit and the distant rhythm that had become so familiar she barely noticed it anymore: scissors. Faint, rhythmic, from somewhere below. Snip. Snip. Snip. She had been hearing them since her first night at Thornfield, a phantom rhythm that Grace Poole attributed to the house settling, that Adèle claimed not to hear at all.
Jane ignored the scissors. She was thinking about a file.

Helen's file. The one that had arrived in her phone like a grenade with the pin already pulled: *Bertha Mason — former Thornfield designer. Disappeared 5 years ago.* The photo had shown a woman with dark eyes and a serious mouth, mid-thirties at the time of disappearance, no body found, no forwarding address, no further inquiry. A closed file. A shut door.
But the timeline matched.

The biodegradable silk collection had debuted four years and ten months ago — two months after Bertha Mason had "disappeared." And the hand that made these garments was the same hand that had been working in Edward Rochester's name for at least five years. Jane had traced its evolution across collections, watched it refine and deepen and grow more assured. She had attributed its invisibility to professional discretion, the ghostwriter's compact. But ghostwriters weren't prisoners. Ghostwriters chose.
Bertha Mason didn't disappear.
She was here.

Somewhere in this château — in a room Jane hadn't found, behind a door she hadn't tried, in a space that existed in the blueprints she hadn't seen — a woman had been sewing SOS messages into couture garments for years. Sewing them and sending them out into the world, tucked inside seams that nobody would turn inside out, carried on the bodies of women who wore Thornfield and never suspected that their clothes were whispering *help me, help me, help me.*
Hoping — against all reason, against all probability — that someone would read the fabric instead of just wearing it.
Jane read fabric.

The realization moved through her like a change in temperature, a shift in the air that rearranged everything it touched. She had been drawn to Thornfield by a hand she couldn't name. She had stayed because the work demanded it. She had been given the archive because no one else cared about construction — they cared about spectacle, about surface, about the name on the label. Nobody turned a garment inside out. Nobody traced a seam that shouldn't exist.
Nobody but her.
She was made to find this.

Jane stood. Her knees ached from the stone floor. She began to fold the garments — carefully, precisely, the way she had found them. Each one went back on its hanger, under its muslin shroud, in its place on the rack. She smoothed the muslin, adjusted the hang, checked the spacing. When she was done, the archive looked exactly as it had when she entered. No trace. No evidence that anyone had found the messages buried in the seams.

Before she re-shrouded each piece, she photographed the red thread. Her phone filled with close-ups: the dots, the dashes, the crimson cotton burning against cream silk. She photographed the seam positions, noted which seam in which garment, created a mental map that she would write down only when she was away from this place, away from the walls that might have ears and the floors that might have eyes.
She was reaching for the last garment — number forty-seven, a sheath dress in pale ivory, the final piece on the rack — when she paused.

The interior lining. She had checked it already, found the HELP code in the side seam, documented it, moved on. But something made her look again. Call it instinct. Call it the particular obsession of a woman who knew that the most important detail was always the one you almost missed.
She held the loupe to the inner lining, near the hem, where the fabric folded and the stitching was dense enough to hide anything. And there, in a seam she had already examined once, she found it.
Red thread. But not HELP.
She traced the dots and dashes: dash-dot. N. ---. O. T. Pause — the thread skipped a stitch, a gap that served as a word break. Dash-dot-dash-dot. C. R. A. Z. Y.
NOT CRAZY.

The two words hit her like a physical blow. She felt them land in her chest, in her throat, behind her eyes. The loupe trembled — her hand, finally, her hand — and she steadied it against the fabric and read it again.
NOT CRAZY.

Whoever made this anticipated it. She anticipated it. She knew — with the terrible clarity of a woman who had been told she was unstable, that she had broken down, that she had gone mad — she knew exactly what they would say about her. Exactly what they HAD said. *She couldn't handle the pressure. She had a breakdown. She went mad.* The narrative that had closed the file, sealed the door, ended the inquiry.
NOT CRAZY.

She wasn't crazy. She was a prisoner. Locked away in this château, still making the garments that bore another man's name, still sewing her truth into seams that no one would read — and defending herself, in advance, against the lie that would be told. Not crazy. The words were a pre-emptive strike against the story that would erase her. They were the most heartbreaking two words Jane had ever read, because they meant someone had needed to write them.

Jane folded the ivory sheath. She placed it on its hanger. She drew the muslin over it like a shroud. She documented the NOT CRAZY message with a hand that did not shake, not now, not anymore, because anger had replaced shock and anger was a steadier fuel.

She put the loupe away. The cord settled against her collarbone, the small lens cool through her shirt. She looked around the archive — the rack of garments, the felt table, the institutional light, the stone walls — and the room seemed different now. The same space, but charged. The garments weren't just beautiful anymore. They were testimony. Every seam a syllable. Every thread a sentence. Forty-seven garments, and every one of them a letter from a woman the world had been told did not exist.
The archive was quiet.
Too quiet.
The hum of the climate unit. The faint tick of temperature gauges. And—
Nothing.
The scissors had stopped.

She had been hearing them for days — the faint, rhythmic snipping from somewhere below, from the floors she hadn't accessed, from the parts of the château that didn't appear on the tour. Snip. Snip. Snip. A constant rhythm, like a heartbeat, like a clock. She had stopped noticing them the way you stop noticing rain, the way you stop noticing your own breathing. They were the sound of Thornfield, and Thornfield was always making that sound.
Until now.
Now the archive was silent. The rhythm was gone. The scissors had ceased mid-cut, mid-breath, mid-sentence — and the absence was louder than the sound had ever been.
As if the person holding them knew.
As if she had felt the shift — some change in the air, some vibration through the stone — and stopped cutting because stopping was a message too.
The ghost heard her.

Jane stood in the silence of the archive, surrounded by garments that whispered, and felt the stillness press against her eardrums like deep water. The cold was in her bones now, the kind of cold that isn't temperature but knowledge — the chill of understanding something you cannot unknow.

She thought of the ivory sheath on the rack, the NOT CRAZY stitched into its hem. She thought of the forty-seven HELP messages hidden in seams that the world would never turn inside out. She thought of Bertha Mason, dark eyes and serious mouth, sewing her truth into couture with red cotton and a prayer, and she thought:
*I hear you.*
*I'm coming.*

The silence held. The scissors did not resume. And somewhere below, in a room Jane had not yet found, a woman waited — not with hope, perhaps, because hope has a shelf life and five years is a long time, but with attention. With the razor alertness of someone who has just felt the lock turn.
Jane pocketed her phone, adjusted the archive rack so the hangers were perfectly spaced, and walked to the door. Her footsteps echoed once, twice, and then the archive swallowed them.
Behind her, the garments hung in their muslin shrouds, still and cold and full of red thread.
In front of her, the corridor stretched toward the main house, toward the stairs, toward the floors she had not been shown.
The scissors stayed silent.
The ghost was listening.
And now, so was Jane.
